author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Did you even try to Google that? Bang this into a Google search prompt: “which version of tls supports quantum resistant algorithms?”

author: sky.cactus
content: My trust of the people in this room is much higher than my trust of Google results these days.  That being said, you are right that this is probably something more appropriate for an LLM chat, so I'll follow-up there.

author: _shemnon
content: There are some builds of TLS 1.3 that support hybrid x-wing style protocols, which are an EC algo (X25519, P256, P384) with ML-KEM (768 or 1024) - but like all tls you need to limit your accepted suites to prevent protocol downgrade by your counterpart.  TLS 1.2 is all EC IIRC

author: _shemnon
content: And PQC only matters durrng the handshake, AES (at 256 bits) is already quantum secure

author: bridge-bot
content:

> <_shemnon> There are some builds of TLS 1.3 that support hybrid x-wing style protocols, which are an EC algo (X25519, P256, P384) with ML-KEM (768 or 1024) - but like all tls you need to limit your accepted suites to prevent protocol downgrade by your counterpart.  TLS 1.2 is all EC IIRC
Also authentication still relies on the certificates which are RSA/ECDSA so no full quantum requests

ahh jinx

author: _shemnon
content: Certs can be pure-pq or hybrid in 1.3, that is something you need to ensure if you are making a fully resistant path.  Browser support is still a work in progress.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<potuz0> re: **@postquantum_bot**
> <_shemnon> Certs can be pure-pq or hybrid in 1.3, that is something you need to ensure if you are making a fully resistant path.  Browser support is still a work in progress.
yes in theory 1.3 accepts pq certs, but the whole PKI ecosystem needs to change.

author: _shemnon
content: I think that's the signal that needs to be surfaced.  Solutions exist, they are not being used.  Add WiFi gear to that list too, as WPA3 is entirely EC dependent.

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Google is part of the supporters of the ISRG who run Let's Encrypt. I'm assuming that by 2029, that infra will have been hardened, same for Chrome and other browsers

author: bridge-bot
content:
<yorickdowne> "In the next few years at the latest", as candidates are vetted

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/post-quantum-crypto-roadmap/245173

author: bridge-bot
content: <shemnon> Diffusion of use is a major concern.  For certs it's easy because they expire and a coordinated "PQ-only" mandate for new certs can be created.  For optional stuff like WiFi? That's the tail risk.  The way this translates into Eth is that consensus and blobs will be fairly short lived transitions (built in protocol deadlines), but the end user accounts will take forever to migrate without some serious incentives or heavy handed "No EC past 2035" rules like OP is imposing.

author: bridge-bot
content:
<yorickdowne> Your saving grace for a while there is going to be the cost of attack. And yes, WiFi needs to move - which on the corporate and consumer side only happens when new gen gear is out, with a one-to-two gen lag. 

WiFi 9 is slated to get PQ  cryptography. That's in the 2030s.

Expect that to roll out gradually over 10 years after that

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> WiFi 6 already supports group key rotation. An org that has a reason to worry about an attack against their WiFi (nation states and their defense contractors, for example) could do key rotation now, and hop to WiFi 9 as soon as gear is out by their vendor of choice (Cisco, Juniper, Ruckus, etc etc)

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Whether they actually will - well, let's say IT departments aren't always on the ball :/

author: bridge-bot
content: <shemnon> key rotation doesn't stop HNDL, it just makes it more expensive.

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Correct. Or it can stop it if it rotates faster than current hardware can break. As far as we can tell no current hardware can break.

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> And with a say 10% chance of "quantum day" by 2032, WiFi 9 would be there in time

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> Make it too expensive to bother now; upgrade when you can; don't worry if your secrets are worth peanuts

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> And "a few million" is peanuts compared to the initial cost of a quantum break

author: bridge-bot
content: <shemnon> HNDL doesn't worry about live decryption, you "decrypt later"

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> For some value of "stop", obviously .. yeah

author: bridge-bot
content: <yorickdowne> That. So, still, world is not ending.
